content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY                                GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor


CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
631 HOWARD STREET, 4TH FLOOR
SAN FRANCISCO, CA  94105
(415) 543-8555
                                                                    JAN 30 9:49 AM '90

                                                                    January 26, 1990


Catherine Donovan
Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Ms. Donovan:

Subject:  Request for Advice Under Political Reform Act

Since 1977 I have been an employee of the California Coastal Commission,
where I am now a coastal program analyst III.  My resignation becomes
effective January 30, 1990, and on February 1, 1990, I will assume the
position of environmental manager for the Port of Oakland.  By this letter I
request your written advice concerning any potential conflict of interest in
connection with my past duties at the Coastal Commission and my future
duties at the Port.  Relevant background information and several specific
questions are set forth below.

Since 1981, my principal duties have involved the Commission's federal
consistency program.  This program is based upon the Federal Coastal Zone
Management Act (CZMA), which gives the Commission limited regulatory
authority in relation to federal and federally permitted activities that
affect the coastal zone.  Since 1980, I have supervised several junior
coastal program analysts working in the federal consistency program.  Either
in my supervisorial role or directly I have been involved in federal
consistency review of numerous projects.  Those which have involved the Port
of Oakland are discussed below.

Throughout my tenure in the federal consistency program, I have also
worked on a variety of special assignments for the Coastal Commission.
These have generally involved matters touching upon my areas of special
interest and expertise: water quality, wetlands, shoreline processes, and
hydraulic engineering.  The projects have included participation in a
wetlands task force established by the Commission to survey past actions of
the Commission and provide guidance for future actions of the Commission on
wetland-related matters.

In connection with both my federal consistency and special project
assignments, I have been involved in the Commission's participation in
informal multi-agency efforts to examine and resolve issues associated with
disposal of dredge materials from San Francisco Bay, including those from
the Port of Oakland.  Although the Bay is not within the jurisdiction

Catherine Donovan
January 26, 1990
Page 2


of the Coastal Commission (the jurisdictional boundary between the coastal
zone of the Commission and the Bay Conservation and Development Commission
(BCDC) is roughly at the mouth of the San Francisco Bay), disposal of
materials dredged from the Bay may require transport through the
Commission's coastal zone and may occur within that area or so close as to
create impacts within the Commission's coastal zone. My participation in
these informal processes on behalf of the Commission has included in-house
planning and discussions and attendance at discussion meetings and public
hearings involving BCDC, water agencies, the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers,
the Environmental Protection Agency, the Port of Oakland, local governments,
commercial and sports fishing representatives, and others. These informal
activities have taken place before, during, and after the specific
proceedings discussed below and have been separate from them, although
related in general subject matter. In part these informal activities have
focused on issues associated with the perceived need for designation of a
permanent marine disposal site offshore San Francisco, pursuant to Section
102 of the Marine Protection, Research, and Sanctuaries Act (MPRSA).

My work for the Commission has included specific proceedings involving
the Port of Oakland. These have arisen in connection with the currently
proposed Oakland Inner and Outer Harbor Deep Draft Navigation Improvement
Project ("the Project"), which is sponsored jointly by the Port of Oakland
and the Corps of Engineers. The Project involves dredging of the entrance
channel to the harbor to deepen the channel. Because the dredge material
was proposed to be transported through, and dumped in or near waters under
the jurisdiction of the Coastal Commission, the Commission became involved
in the Project.

The first phase of the Commission's involvement concerned a "negative
determination" prepared by the Corps and submitted to the Commission in
September, 1987. The negative determination process, described in 15 C.F.R.
930.35(d), is available for projects with de minimis impacts on the coastal
zone. The Corps' negative determination and the accompanying Draft
Environmental Impact Statement (DEIS) covered only the first phase of the
project, which was described as involving removal of 2.7-million cubic yards
of material from an established dredge disposal site near Alcatraz Island in
San Francisco Bay and disposal of that material at a marine site know as
M-1, approximately 10 miles offshore. This temporary site had been
designated under Section 103 of the MPRSA. The DEIS indicated that material
dredged from the harbor would be deposited at the Alcatraz site. This
latter aspect of the activity was not covered by the negative determination
nor in any way made a part of the proceeding before the Commission. The
overall Project was described as a Corps activity, even though the Port
would help pay for it and would be its principal beneficiary. I supervised
the work of a staff member assigned to review the submittal, and as a result
of that staff work, on October 7, 1987, the Commission staff concurred with
the Corps' negative determination. The Port played no active role in the
negative determination process, to my knowledge. All of the Commission's
contacts on the matter were with the Corps, and not with the Port.

Catherine Donovan
January 26, 1990
Page 3

     The next phase of the Commission's involvement comprised informal review
of a Final Environmental Impact Statement (FEIS). The FEIS showed that the
portions of the Project which engaged Commission participation had changed
very substantially. Dredge material disposal was proposed at a completely
different marine site, also a temporary site designated under Section 103 of
the MPRSA. Termed B1 (sometimes termed B1B), the site was farther offshore
(about 12 miles) and farther south. The material proposed to be dumped in
the ocean would come directly from dredging of the Port of Oakland channel,
rather than from the Alcatraz site, as initially proposed. Further, the
Project had been segmented into two phases, Phase I involving 500,000 cubic
yards of material, and Phase II involving another 6.5-million cubic yards.
Only Phase I was covered by the FEIS. Again, the FEIS described the Project
as a Corps activity. At this stage of the Commission's involvement, there
was no formal submittal from the Corps which required formal action of the
Commission or its staff. Rather, Commission staff response was in the form
of a comment letter, dated April 22, 1988, prepared under my supervision.
The letter did not ask for federal consistency review of Phase I. It did
ask that the Commission participate in review of later phases of the Project.

     The third phase of the Commission's participation has involved pending
litigation. In late May or early June of 1988, it came to light that Phase
I of the Project, involving dredging and dumping of 500,000 cubic yards at
the B1 site (offshore Half Moon Bay in San Mateo County), was to be carried
out by the Port itself. On May 13 the County of San Mateo filed a lawsuit
against the Port and the Coastal Commission seeking to enjoin Phase I until
the Port had submitted and the Commission had approved a federal consistency
certification pursuant to Section 307(c)(3)(A) of the CZMA. The trial court
issued the injunction, and the decision is on appeal. County of San Mateo
v. Port of Oakland, California Coastal Commission, No. 329870, Superior
Court for County of San Mateo decided June 9, 1988. In my supervisorial
role and directly, I have participated in this legal proceeding by providing
information to the Commission's attorneys and taking part in some
discussions concerning conduct of the litigation.

     In the course of this dispute, additional information has come to light
concerning possible impacts upon coastal zone resources from ocean disposal
of dredge materials. As a result, the Commission has taken the position
that, barring a contrary final adjudication, federal consistency review by
the Commission will be required for transport and disposal in or near the
coastal zone of all of the subject dredge materials from the Port of Oakland
- irrespective of how the overall project is ultimately phased and whether
it is carried out by the Port or the Corps.

     As environmental manager at the Port of Oakland, I will head a staff of
12 persons. The responsibilities of my department will include analysis of
environmental issues affecting the Port, advice and participation in Port
decisions, and implementation of environmental requirements. Please advise
me on the following questions:

Catherine Donovan
January 26, 1990
Page 4

1. If disposal of dredge materials in connection with the Project is the
subject of federal consistency review by the Commission, must my
participation on behalf of the Port be limited in any way? Specifically,
would limitations apply:

a. If Phase I, dredge and disposal of 500,000 cubic yards at site B1,
is carried out by the Port, which prepares and submits to the Commission
a federal consistency certification under Section 307(c)(3) of the
CZMA. (This is the current formulation of Phase I, as it has been
presented in the above-described litigation.)

b. If Phase I changes in some particular, including selection of a new
disposal site, significant change in the volume of dredge material
involved, or assumption of lead responsibility by the Corps. In the
latter case, consistency review would proceed under Section 307(c)(1) of
the CZMA, which involves somewhat different procedural requirements.
The Corps would submit the consistency determination to the Commission,
but barring a conflict of interest, I would likely participate in its
preparation.

c. If Phase II of the Project, (dredge and disposal of the remaining
6.5-million cubic yards) is carried out. As contemplated all along, it
seems likely that this portion of the Project will by executed by the
Corps, and the consistency determination will be prepared and submitted
to the Commission by the Corps. However, ordinarily the Port's
environmental manager could be expected to be involved in both of these
aspects of Phase II.

2. May I participate on behalf of the Port in planning for permanent
designation of a permanent disposal site for dredge materials, including
those from the Port of Oakland, pursuant to Section 102 of the MPRSA? Is
any such otherwise allowable participation limited with respect to
appearances before the Coastal Commission? (Permanent site designation,
which is under control of the Environmental Protection Agency, will
require federal consistency review by the Coastal Commission.)

3. If you advise that I must disqualify myself from participation in any of
the above activities of the Port, what steps must I take to adequately
withdraw from participation? Must all members of my staff refrain from
participation in any matters on which I am disqualified? If not, must my
management duties be modified with respect to any of my staff persons
involved in a project on which I am disqualified?

4. My duties at the Commission have familiarized me with the Commission's
position on various issues which may become germane in future Commission
proceedings involving the Port of Oakland. These include the effect of the
1988 Presidential Proclamation extending the United States Territorial Sea
upon Coastal Commission jurisdiction offshore California, and various

Catherine Donovan
January 26, 1990
Page 5

aspects of the Commission's federal consistency program. What, if any,
constraints must I observe in utilizing this knowledge on behalf of the Port?

I am enclosing key written materials mentioned above. If you require
further materials or information, please feel free to contact me at
415-272-1175 or Mary Hudson, deputy chief counsel, at the Coastal Commission
(543-8555). Because planning for the Project is on-going at the Port of
Oakland and will be an important aspect of my responsibilities there, I need
your reply at the earliest possible time.

Sincerely,

[signature]

JAMES MCGRATH

Enclosures

151IL

STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY

GEORGE DEUKWEJIAN, Governor

CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
631 HOWARD STREET, 4TH FLOOR
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94105
(415) 543-8555

October 7, 1987


Colonel Galen H. Yanagihara
Department of the Army
San Francisco District, Corps of Engineers
211 Main Street
San Francisco, CA 94105-1905

RE:     CD-49-87, Proposed deepening of Oakland Harbor channels, Alameda
        County


Dear Colonel Yanagihara:

The Coastal Commission staff has received the material you submitted
concerning the above referenced project. The Commission staff concurs with
the Corps of Engineers conclusion that the proposed project will not have a
direct affect land and water uses within the California Coastal Commission's
jurisdiction. Thus pursuant to Section 930.35 (d)(1) of the National Oceanic
and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Federal Consistency regulations no
consistency review from the Coastal Commission is required. However, the
project could impact resources within the jurisdiction of the San Francisco
Bay Conservation and Development Commission, and require consistency review
from that agency.


        Sincerely,

        Peter Douglas [signature]
        Executive Director


cc. Central Coast District
    NOAA
    OCRM
    Governor's Washington, D.C. Office
    Department of Water Resources
    BCDC

PD/JRR
0109p

STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY                                                   GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
631 HOWARD STREET, 4TH FLOOR
SAN FRANCISCO, CA  94105
(415) 543-8555                                                                                        June 6, 1988


Mr. Walter A. Abernathy
Executive Director
Port of Oakland
66 Jack London Square
P.O. Box 2064
Oakland, CA  94604

                    RE:  Oakland Inner and Outer Harbor Dredging Project

Dear Mr. Abernathy:

     I am writing concerning the Oakland inner and outer harbor dredging
project and the need for review of that project's first phase for consistency
with the California Coastal Management Program (CCMP), which has
been approved under the Federal Coastal Zone Management Act (CZMA) and is
administered by the Coastal Commission (excepting that portion under the
jurisdiction of the Bay Conservation and Development Commission), provides
that certain federal permit and license activities which affect land and water
uses in the coastal zone are subject to the federal consistency provisions of
Section 1456(c)(3) of the CZMA. As required by federal regulations
implementing the CZMA, the CCMP includes a list of such federal license and
permit activities subject to consistency review. Included are permits for the
transportation of dredged materials for the purpose of dumping into ocean
waters, under Section 103 of the Marine Protection, Research, and Sanctuary
Act of 1972. A copy of the list is enclosed.

     As you may know, in April the Coastal Commission staff reviewed and
concurred in a determination by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers that the
first portion of the project would not have adverse direct effects upon the
coastal zone. Background materials presented to the Commission with the Corps'
negative determination did not include information which has since come to
light concerning possible impacts of the dredged material on fishery
resources. After this new information began to come in, the Commission's
concerns over the potential coastal zone impacts of this activity were
expressed in my April 22 letter to Corps district engineer Col. Galen
Yanagihara. (Copy enclosed.) Even since the date of that letter, we have
obtained additional information about possible adverse effects of the project,
including the possibility that some of the spoils to be hauled through the
coastal zone and dumped in fishing areas may be toxic. Since our initial
review of this project we have also learned that the first phase of the
project, instead of being a federal project as we had thought, is to be
carried out by the Port of Oakland pursuant to a Section 103 permit issued by
the Corps.

     In light of these changed circumstances, it appears that this first
phase of the dredge disposal project is likely to have impacts upon coastal

Mr. Walter A. Abernathy
June 6, 1988
Page 2


zone resources and must be reviewed for consistency with the California
Coastal Management Program. The Section 103 permit authorizes transportation,
with the attendant risks of spillage, of dredged material through coastal zone
waters subject to Coastal Commission jurisdiction. Under Section 930.53 of
the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration regulations implementing
the CZMA (16 C.F.R. Part 930), federal consistency review is required for such
a federal permit activity.

     The Section 103 permit also authorizes disposal of the dredged material
at a site seaward of the coastal boundary in an area used by fishermen
operating from within the coastal zone. Although listed Federal permit
activities taking place outside the coastal zone are not automatically subject
to consistency review, the CCMP provides that a consistency certification will
be required in cases where the activity could have a substantial effect on
land and water uses in the coastal zone. Commission staff has received a
number of communications indicating that the chosen disposal site is an
especially valuable fishery resource, that its fishery values will be
destroyed by the proposed deposition of dredged material, and that this loss
will significantly impact the San Mateo County fishing industry. The
information we have received to date indicates that this proposed dumping
activity may indeed have a substantial effect on land and water uses in the
coastal zone.

     Accordingly, I request that, for the proposed transportation and dumping
activities authorized by the Section 103 permit, the Port of Oakland prepare a
certification regarding the consistency of these activities with the
California Coastal Management Program. We will make every effort to expedite
our review of this certification. To discuss details of timing and
preparation of the consistency certification, I suggest that your staff
contact Jim Raives of this office, who has been assigned to this matter.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        PETER M. DOUGLAS
                                        Executive Director


cc:  Michael Murphy, Esq.
     Roger Beers, Esq.
     Stanley P. Hebert, Esq.
     Gregory Antone, Esq.
     Colonel Galen H. Yanagihara
     Honorable Quentin Kopp
     Matt Rodriguez, Deputy Attorney General

Enclosures

STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY                    GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
631 HOWARD STREET, 4TH FLOOR
SAN FRANCISCO, CA  94105
(415) 543-8555
Hearing Impaired/TDD (415) 896-1825

April 22, 1988


Colonel Galen H. Yanagihara
San Francisco District
Corps of Engineers
211 Main Street
San Francisco, CA  94105-1905

Subject: Oakland Inner and Outer Harbor dredging, CD-47-87

Dear Colonel Yanagihara:

On October 7, 1987, I concurred with the Corps of Engineers negative determination for the Oakland harbor dredging project. This concurrence was based on our review of the Draft Supplement I to the Final Environmental Impact Statement, Oakland Outer and Oakland Inner Harbor Deep-Draft Navigation Improvements, Alameda County (the "Draft Supplement"). That Draft Supplement provided for the dredging of 2.7 million cubic yards of material already in the marine environment near Alcatraz Island and the disposal of that material offshore and outside of the coastal zone at a site referred to as "M-1" in the document. Disposal would be pursuant to the Marine Protection, Research and Sanctuaries Act of 1972 and thus would be subject to analysis with respect to fish direct toxic effects and bioaccumulation. The Draft Supplement had little information about the effect of ocean disposal on commercial fishing, including only the single statement that "fishing that may occur in the vicinity of or at the nearshore disposal and modified as a result of changes to the bottom substrate." Extensive monitoring of the disposal site was also proposed.

On March 31, 1988 we received copies of (1) the Deep-Draft Navigation Improvements Design Memorandum Number 1, General Design and Final Supplement I to the Environmental Impact Statement, Alameda County (the "Final Supplement"); (2) the Addendum to the Final Supplement I to the Environmental Impact Statements Oakland Outer and Oakland Inner Harbor Deep-Draft Navigation Improvements, Alameda County, California (the "Addendum") 1988 (the "Notice"); and (3) Notice of Availability Design Memorandum Number 1 and Final Supplement I to the Environmental Impact Statements Oakland Outer and Oakland Inner Harbor Deep-Draft Navigation Improvements, Alameda County, California (the "Notice"). Those reports provide for a somewhat different project. The Phase I project would involve the dredging of approximate 500,000 cubic yards of material from Oakland harbor, use of a clamshell dredge, transport of that material using hoppers, and disposal of the material at a site known as B1 further offshore than the 1M site designated in the Draft Supplement. The reports indicate that no contaminated material would be disposed of in the marine environment. Apparently additional testing is now underway to accurately identify the limits of uncontaminated material, and any contaminated dredge spoil will be placed in a land disposal site. The statement is also made in the Final Supplement that "Direct ocean disposal at B1 is selected because the disposal site has the least value to the local fishing industry." The Notice concludes that ocean disposal "...is not

-2-

expected to have a long term impact on commercial marine resources." It is
our understanding that the designation of site B1 is presently limited to the
Phase I project, and that subsequent supplements will be prepared for any
further use of site B1 (or any other site) for any additional dredge spoils
disposal beyond Phase I.

We applaud the intent of the Corps to select a marine disposal site that
minimizes adverse impacts on commercial fishing as well as other marine
resources. However, we have recently received a number of inquiries from the
commercial fishing industry that convince us that this goal may not have been
realized. In reviewing the document with these comments in mind, it appears
that the decisions about commercial fishing impacts were based upon a
minimal amount of information which is no longer current. Fishing data from the
1976-77 season is presented in attachment B to the Addendum and is apparently
the only source of information used in reaching the conclusions about impacts
on commercial fishing in the Final Supplement, Addendum, and Notice.

It is our understanding that additional analysis will be completed prior to
any decisions on the site for disposal of the remaining 6.5 million cubic
yards of dredge materials, with the results of that analysis reported in an
additional supplement and further meetings. We respectfully request to be
involved in the discussions, analyses, and decisions associated with the
subsequent disposal activities. The commercial fishing industry is a major
coastal resource and an important element of California's coastal economy.
Significant impacts to that industry, even if they result from activities
outside the coastal zone, may well directly affect the viability of this
important coastal resource. We believe it would be in everyone's best
interest if representatives of that industry are also involved in the
decision-making process so that their views can be considered. In that way,
current information the industry has about the nature and value of the fishing
resource in the affected area can be evaluated and factored into any final
decision on subsequent use of site B1 for dredge spoils disposal.

Very truly yours,

PETER DOUGLAS
Executive Director

cc: Environmental Protection Agency

SECTION SEVEN

7. LOCAL COOPERATION

7.1. Coordination with Local Interests

The Department of the Army has entered into a local cooperation
agreement with the Port of Oakland for the construction and
maintenance of the Oakland Harbor Deep Water Ship Channels. The
Local Cooperation Agreement is presented in total in Appendix D.

7.2. Local Cooperation Agreement

The obligations of the local sponsor are specified in Article
II of the Local Cooperation Agreement between the Department of the
Army and the Port of Oakland for Construction and Maintenance of
Navigation Improvements at Oakland Outer and Inner Harbors, which
reads in part as follows:

Article II - OBLIGATIONS OF PARTIES

a. The Local Sponsor shall provide and maintain, at its own
expense, all project facilities other than those for general
navigation, including dredged depths in berthing areas and local
access channels serving the general navigation features
commensurate with those in related general navigation features.

b. The Local Sponsor shall provide to the Government all
lands, easements, and rights-of-way, including dredged material
disposal areas, and perform all relocations or alterations of
facilities other than utilities governed by paragraph c. below
(except relocations or alterations of highway and railway bridges),
determined by the Government to be necessary for construction,
operation, or maintenance of the project.

c. The Local Sponsor shall perform or assure performance of
all utility relocations, including the Navy sanitary sewer, or
alterations determined by the Government to be necessary for
construction, operation, or maintenance of the project.

d. The Local Sponsor shall provide, operate and maintain
without cost to the Government adequate public terminals and
transfer facilities open to all on equal terms.

e. To the extent it is legally empowered to do so, the
LocalSponsor shall prohibit erection of any structure within 125
feet of the project channel lines.

f. The Local Sponsor shall provide, during the period of
construction, a cash contribution equal to the following
percentages of the total cost of construction of the general
navigation facilities assigned to commercial navigation:

10 percent of the costs attributable to the portion of the
project which has a depth not in excess of 20 feet:

-55-

25 percent of the costs attributable to the portion of the
project which has a depth in excess of 20 feet but not in excess of
45 feet:

g. The Local Sponsor shall repay with interest, over a period
not to exceed 30 years following completion of the general
navigation features of the project or separable element thereof, an
additional 0 to 10 percent of the total cost of construction of
general navigation facilities assigned to commercial navigation,
depending on the value, as calculated under Article IV hereof, of
items provided pursuant to paragraph b. of this Article. If the
credit allowed for such items is less than 10 percent of the total
cost of construction of general navigation features, the Local
Sponsor shall repay a percentage of such local cost equal to the
difference between 10 percent of the total cost and the percentage
of the total cost represented by the value of such items. If the
credit allowed is equal to or greater than 10 percent of said total
cost, the Local Sponsor shall not be required to repay any
additional percentage of the total cost.

h. The Government, subject to and using funds provided by the
Local Sponsor and appropriated by Congress, shall expeditiously
construct the general navigation features of the project (including
relocations or alterations of highway and railway bridges),
applying those procedures usually followed or applied in Federal
projects, pursuant to Federal laws, regulations, and policies. The
Local Sponsor shall be afforded the opportunity to review and
comment on all relevant plans and specifications prior to the
issuance of invitations for bids. The Local Sponsor also shall be
afforded the opportunity to review and comment on all modifications
and change orders prior to the issuance to the contractor the
maximum extent feasible. The Local Sponsor will be supplied with a
copy of all modifications and change orders. The Government will
consider the views of the sponsor, but award of the contracts and
performance of the work thereunder shall be exclusively within the
control of the Government.

i. The Government shall operate and maintain the general
navigation features of the project.

j. The Government shall acquire such interests as are
necessary to construct, operate and maintain those portions of the
Project lying within the City of Alameda. The Government will
coordinate the selection of the appraiser with the Local Sponsor
and copies of the approved appraisal reports will be provided the
Local Sponsor.

ARTICLE III - LANDS, FACILITIES, AND RELOCATION ASSISTANCE

a. Prior to the advertisement of any construction contract,
the Local Sponsor shall furnish to the Government all lands,
easements and rights-of-way, including suitable borrow and dredged
material disposal areas, as may be determined by the Government to
be necessary for construction, operation, and maintenance of the
general navigation features, and shall furnish to the Government
-56-

evidence supporting the Local Sponsor's legal authority to grant
rights-of-entry to such lands.

b. The Local Sponsor shall provide or pay to the Government
the full cost of providing all retaining dikes, wastewiers,
bulkheads, and embankments, including all monitoring features and
stilling basins, determined by the Government to be necessary for
construction, operation, or maintenance of the general navigation
features.

c. The Local Sponsor shall reimburse the Government for all
costs associated with the Government's acquisition of such
interests as are necessary to construct, operate and maintain those
portions of the Project lying within the City of Alameda.

d. Upon notification from the Government, and subject to the
Government's acquisition of interests in the City of Alameda and
the Government's furnishing to the Local Sponsor rights-of-entry
pursuant to said interests, the Local Sponsor shall accomplish all
necessary alterations and relocations of buildings, highways,
railroads, storm drains, and other facilities, structures, and
improvements.

e. Upon notification from the Government, the Local Sponsor
shall perform or assure performance of all necessary alterations
and relocations of pipelines, cables, and other utilities,
including the Navy sanitary sewer. Nothing herein shall be deemed
to affect the ability of the Local Sponsor to seek compensation
from other non-Federal entities for costs it incurs under this
paragraph.

f. The Local Sponsor shall comply with the applicable
provisions of the Uniform Relocations Assistance and Real Property
Acquisition Policies Act of 1970, Public Law 91-646, Approved
January 2, 1971, in acquiring lands, easements, and rights-of-way
for construction and subsequent operation and maintenance of the
project, and inform all affected persons of applicable benefits,
policies, and procedures in connection with said Act.

NOTE: Article II, Paragraph c. indicates that relocation of the
Navy sanitary sewer is a local responsibility. It has since been
determined that this is a project cost and cost shared similarly
(75% Federal and 25% non-Federal).

-57-

embedding.qa_text: **STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY** **CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION** 631 HOWARD STREET, 4TH FLOOR SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94105 (415) 543-8555 January 26, 1990 Catherine Donovan Chief Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804 Dear Ms. Donovan: Subject: Request for Advice Under Political Reform Act Since 1977 I have been an employee of the California Coastal Commission, where I am now a coastal program analyst III. My resignation becomes effective January 30, 1990, and on February 1, 1990, I will assume the position of environmental manager for the Port of Oakland. By this letter I request your written advice concerning any potential conflict of interest in connection with my past duties at the Coastal Commission and my future duties at the Port. Relevant background information and several specific questions are set forth below. Since 1981, my principal duties have involved the Commission's federal consistency program. This program is based upon the Federal Coastal Zone Management Act (CZMA), which gives the Commission limited regulatory authority in relation to federal and federally permitted activities that affect the coastal zone. Since 1980, I have supervised several junior coastal program analysts working in the federal consistency program. Either in my supervisory role or directly I have been involved in federal consistency review of numerous projects. Those which have involved the Port of Oakland are discussed below. Throughout my tenure in the federal consistency program, I have also worked on a variety of special assignments for the Coastal Commission. These have generally involved matters touching upon my areas of special interest and expertise: water quality, wetlands, shoreline processes, and hydraulic engineering. The projects have included participation in a wetlands task force established by the Commission to survey past actions of the Commission and provide guidance for future actions of the Commission on wetland-related matters. In connection with both my federal consistency and special project assignments, I have been involved in the Commission's participation in informal multi-agency efforts to examine and resolve issues associated with disposal of dredge materials from San Francisco Bay, including those from the Port of Oakland. Although the Bay is not within the jurisdiction of the Coastal Commission (the jurisdictional boundary between the coastal zone of the Commission and the Bay Conservation and Development Commission (BCDC) is roughly at the mouth of the San Francisco Bay), disposal of materials dredged from the Bay may require transport through the Commission's coastal zone and may occur within that area or so close as to create impacts within the Commission's coastal zone. My participation in these informal processes on behalf of the Commission has included in-house planning and discussions and attendance at discussion meetings and public hearings involving BCDC, water agencies, the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, the Environmental Protection Agency, the Port of Oakland, local governments, commercial and sports fishing representatives, and others. These informal activities have taken place before, during, and after the specific proceedings discussed below and have been separate from them, although related in general subject matter. In part
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION 631 HOWARD STREET, 4TH FLOOR SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94105 (415) 543-8555 JAN 30 9:49 AM '90 January 26, 1990 Catherine Donovan Chief Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804 Dear Ms. Donovan: Subject: Request for Advice Under Political Reform Act Since 1977 I have been an employee of the California Coastal Commission, where I am now a coastal program analyst III. My resignation becomes effective January 30, 1990, and on February 1, 1990, I will assume the position of environmental manager for the Port of Oakland. By this letter I request your written advice concerning any potential conflict of interest in connection with my past duties at the Coastal Commission and my future duties at the Port. Relevant background information and several specific questions are set forth below. Since 1981, my principal duties have involved the Commission's federal consistency program. This program is based upon the Federal Coastal Zone Management Act (CZMA), which gives the Commission limited regulatory authority in relation to federal and federally permitted activities that affect the coastal zone. Since 1980, I have supervised several junior coastal program analysts working in the federal consistency program. Either in my supervisorial role or directly I have been involved in federal consistency review of numerous projects. Those which have involved the Port of Oakland are discussed below. Throughout my tenure in the federal consistency program, I have also worked on a variety of special assignments for the Coastal Commission. These have generally involved matters touching upon my areas of special interest and expertise: water quality, wetlands, shoreline processes, and hydraulic engineering. The projects have included participation in a wetlands task force established by the Commission to survey past actions of the Commission and provide guidance for future actions of the Commission on wetland-related matters. In connection with both my federal consistency and special project assignments, I have been involved in the Commission's participation in informal multi-agency efforts to examine and resolve issues associated with disposal of dredge materials from San Francisco Bay, including those from the Port of Oakland. Although the Bay is not within the jurisdiction Catherine Donovan January 26, 1990 Page 2 of the Coastal Commission (the jurisdictional boundary between the coastal zone of the Commission and the Bay Conservation and Development Commission (BCDC) is roughly at the mouth of the San Francisco Bay), disposal of materials dredged from the Bay may require transport through the Commission's coastal zone and may occur within that area or so close as to create impacts within the Commission's coastal zone. My participation in these informal processes on behalf of the Commission has included in-house planning and discussions and attendance at discussion meetings and public hearings involving BCDC, water agencies, the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, the Environmental Protection Agency, the Port of Oakland, local governments, commercial and sports fishing representatives, and others. These informal activities have taken place before, during, and after the specific proceedings discussed
